food_subgroup: Biryani and rice dishes with meat

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002700). No specific popcorn/crispy chicken biryani subclass exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers chicken biryani as a composite dish. The breadcrumb-fried chicken variant is not separately coded; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for biryani or this specific dish variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Popcorn Chicken Biryani is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous portion of basmati rice topped with several pieces of crispy breadcrumb-coated fried chicken.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Biryani (composite dish) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fried breaded chicken component
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for chicken biryani (cooked basmati rice with spiced chicken). The popcorn/crispy breadcrumb-fried chicken variant adds additional fat and carbohydrate from the batter. Values are adapted by blending ICMR-NIN chicken biryani data (~60% rice component) with USDA SR Legacy fried breaded chicken data (~40% chicken component) to reflect the composite dish. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are weighted estimates. This is an estimated adaptation; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; Basmati rice GI reference from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for popcorn chicken biryani. GI estimated at 58 based on: (1) basmati rice GI of 57–63 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables), which forms the dominant carbohydrate source (~60% of dish by weight); (2) the fried breadcrumb chicken component contributes additional fat which moderately lowers the overall GI relative to plain rice. A composite GI of 58 (medium category) is defensible. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 90.0) / 100 = 52.2, rounded to 52.4 for a 400 g serving providing approximately 90 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1520 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or avoid this dish, as a single 400 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1520 mg sodium — over 65% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from biryani spice blends, salt in the marinade, and the breadcrumb coating.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) and high glycemic load per serving (~52)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish: although basmati rice has a moderate GI relative to other rice varieties, the large serving size delivers approximately 90 g of available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~52, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion control to half a standard serving and pairing with non-starchy vegetables is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from deep-frying and ghee, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish presents a moderate concern due to its saturated fat content (~2.1 g per 100 g, ~8.4 g per serving) from ghee and deep-frying oil, alongside high sodium; occasional consumption in reduced portions is preferable, and preparation methods using less oil and ghee would improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Popcorn Chicken Biryani (also marketed as 'Bullet Biryani') is a contemporary Indian-Singaporean fusion biryani dish in which bite-sized chicken pieces are marinated in spiced yoghurt, coated in a seasoned breadcrumb batter, and deep-fried until golden and crunchy before being layered or served atop long-grain basmati rice cooked with whole biryani spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf), caramelised onions, and ghee. The result is a dish that combines the aromatic, saffron-tinged fragrance of traditional biryani with the textural contrast of crispy fried chicken — a format popularised in Singapore's Indian-Muslim hawker and restaurant scene. The dish is calorie-dense (~195 kcal per 100 g; ~780 kcal per standard 400 g plate), with moderate protein (~10.5 g per 100 g) from the chicken, significant carbohydrate (~22.5 g per 100 g) from the basmati rice and breadcrumb coating, and elevated fat (~7.8 g per 100 g) from deep-frying and ghee. Sodium is high (~380 mg per 100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium), driven primarily by basmati rice, but the glycemic load per full serving is high (~52), requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is typically halal-certified in Singapore's Indian-Muslim establishments and contains gluten (breadcrumbs) and dairy (ghee, yoghurt). It is commonly served with raita (yoghurt dip), sliced onions, and lime wedges.
common_user_queries: Is bullet biryani the same as popcorn chicken biryani?
How many calories in popcorn chicken biryani?
What is the GI of chicken biryani?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for popcorn chicken biryani exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo databases. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken biryani data blended with USDA SR Legacy fried breaded chicken data, weighted by approximate dish composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite adapted estimate.
notes: 1. The input food name 'Popocorn Chicken (Bullet) Biryani' contains a typographical error ('Popocorn'); the canonical name is corrected to 'Popcorn Chicken Biryani'. The alias list preserves the original spelling variants for retrieval purposes. 2. Nutrient values are estimated adaptations; an exact laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this specific dish would upgrade confidence to high. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy sources for this composite dish. 6. The 'Bullet Biryani' marketing name used by some Singapore restaurants refers to the same dish concept and is preserved as an alias. 7. Halal status is inferred from the Indian-Muslim restaurant context (Briyani Hut); individual restaurant certification should be verified independently. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (58 × 90.0 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 52.2, reported as 52.4 after rounding adjustment for available versus total carbohydrate.
